title: FGSM & PGD Attacks
concept: FGSM and PGD are gradient-based algorithms used to calculate adversarial perturbations by backpropagating loss to the input pixels.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main threat of an adversarial patch?

options.text: It can be printed and placed in the real world to fool camera systems
It steals model weights from edge hardware
It corrupts the training database files

exercises.explain: Patches extend adversarial threats to the physical world, manipulating cameras and sensors.

cards.title: Using the model's brain against it
cards.body: When we train a model, we use gradients to update weights and reduce error. What if we run this process in reverse? We can hold the weights constant and use gradients to modify the input pixels to maximize error instead! This is how attacks are created.

cards.title: FGSM & PGD Algorithms
cards.body: Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) is a one-step attack that adds pixel changes in the direction of the gradient sign. Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) is an iterative, multi-step version that is much stronger, finding the optimal noise within a size limit.

cards.title: Hiking up the error mountain
cards.body: Training a model is like hiking down a mountain to find the lowest point (minimum error). Creating an adversarial attack is like hiking UP the mountain as fast as possible to find the peak of error. FGSM takes one giant leap up; PGD takes small, careful steps to find the absolute peak.

cards.title: Generating a PGD attack
cards.body: An auditing system runs PGD on an image classifier. Over 10 iterations, it adjusts the pixels slightly, verifying how the confidence drops. By step 10, the classification confidence falls from 99% to 2%.

cards.title: White-box vs. Black-box attacks
cards.body: You might think an attacker must know your model's exact weights (white-box) to build these attacks. In reality, adversarial attacks are highly transferable: an attack generated on a standard ResNet often successfully fools a completely different MobileNet (black-box)!

cards.title: FGSM vs. PGD Math Steps
cards.body: Comparing single-step and multi-step gradient attacks:

left.text:
Single step optimization
Very fast to compute
Adds noise in sign direction
Easier to defend against

right.text:
Multiple iterative steps
Slower to compute
Keeps noise within small bounds
Extremely strong attack

exercises.prompt: Which algorithm calculates adversarial noise in a single step using the sign of the model's loss gradients?
exercises.explain: FGSM is a fast, single-step attack method that uses input gradients to maximize loss.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) is generally considered a stronger attack than FGSM because it uses multiple iterative steps.
exercises.explain: True. PGD takes multiple steps to find the most damaging perturbations, bypassing simple defenses.

exercises.sentence: If an attack is calculated with full access to the model's weights and architecture, it is a ___ attack.
exercises.explain: White-box attacks assume the attacker has complete knowledge of the target model's parameters and gradients.

exercises.prompt: What does the concept of attack transferability mean in adversarial AI?

options.text: An adversarial image generated to fool one model can often fool a completely different model
Gradients can be copied between the CPU and the NPU
Model weights can be exported to TFLite format easily

exercises.explain: Adversarial examples capture general features that confuse neural structures, allowing attacks to cross model boundaries.

exercises.prompt: You are security-testing an autonomous vehicle detector. You want to simulate the worst-case digital attack to see if your model can withstand it. Which attack generation method should you choose?

options.text: Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) with a very low learning rate.
Adding random Gaussian noise to the validation images.
Running post-training quantization on the weights.

exercises.explain: PGD is widely recognized as the gold standard for white-box adversarial testing, representing the strongest first-order mathematical attack against neural networks.
